description:
This diagram illustrates the process of generating a differential interferogram in Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) for ground deformation monitoring.

1.  An "Interferogram" is derived from two radar acquisitions, conceptually represented by two grey satellite images of a mountain. These acquisitions are characterized by azimuth and range axes. The resulting interferogram (bottom left image) displays concentric phase fringes, primarily containing phase contributions from topography.
2.  A "Synthetic interferogram" is computed based on a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) of the terrain, shown as a colourful 3D model with contour lines, and precise InSAR acquisition parameters. The parameters for this example are listed: Sensor = ERS-1/2, Polarization (Pol.) = VV, Band = C, Wavelength (λ) = 0.0566 m, Frequency (f₀) = 5.3 GHz, Orbit = ascending, Normal Baseline (Bₙ) = 59 m, and Temporal Baseline (Bₜ) = 1 day. This synthetic interferogram (bottom middle image) models the topographic phase component.
3.  The "Synthetic interferogram" is subtracted from the "Interferogram".
4.  The result is the "Differential interferogram" (bottom right image), which displays irregular, speckled phase patterns, indicating residual phase information after the topographic component has been removed. This differential phase primarily reveals ground displacement and atmospheric effects.
